task_description:
# Fix a Typo in an Ancestor Commit with Jujutsu (jj)

## Background
One of the headline features of Jujutsu (jj) is first-class history rewriting: you can edit any commit in the middle of a stack and jj will automatically rebase every descendant on top of the rewritten commit. In this task you must use that capability to correct a typo that was introduced in the OLDEST commit of a four-commit stack.

A colocated jj/git repository already exists at `/home/user/myrepo`. The user identity has already been configured. The repository contains the following chain of commits (oldest to newest), all reachable from the working copy `@`:

1. **Add greeting** (oldest) â€” creates `greeting.txt` whose contents are exactly `Helo World` (note the typo, missing `l` in `Hello`).
2. **Add feature 1** â€” creates `feature1.txt`.
3. **Add feature 2** â€” creates `feature2.txt`.
4. **Add feature 3** â€” creates `feature3.txt`.
5. The current working copy commit `@` is an empty commit on top of "Add feature 3".

## Requirements
- Fix the typo in `greeting.txt` so that it contains exactly `Hello World` (followed by a single trailing newline) in the snapshot of the OLDEST commit (the commit whose description is "Add greeting").
- The four original commit descriptions ("Add greeting", "Add feature 1", "Add feature 2", "Add feature 3") must still exist as a linear chain of ancestors of `@`, in the same order.
- All descendant commits must be automatically rebased on top of the rewritten ancestor (this is jj's default behavior â€” do NOT use `--restore-descendants` or otherwise prevent the rebase).
- After the fix, the working-copy tip `@` must contain `Hello World` (no typo) in `greeting.txt`, and `feature1.txt`, `feature2.txt`, `feature3.txt` must all still exist at `@`.

## Implementation Guide
There are two idiomatic jj approaches; either is acceptable as long as the final state matches the requirements:

**Approach A â€” `jj edit` + edit file + return to tip:**
1. `cd /home/user/myrepo`
2. Move the working copy onto the "Add greeting" commit: `jj edit -r 'description(substring:"Add greeting")'`. In jj 0.38 the `description()` revset function requires an explicit match-kind prefix such as `substring:` or `exact:`.
3. Fix the file: write `Hello World` (with a trailing newline) into `greeting.txt`.
4. Run any jj command (e.g. `jj status`) so jj snapshots the change into the "Add greeting" commit. Descendants will be automatically rebased.
5. Move the working copy back to the top of the (rewritten) chain so that `feature1.txt`, `feature2.txt`, and `feature3.txt` are all present at `@`. For example: `jj edit -r 'description(substring:"Add feature 3")'`, or create a new empty tip on top of "Add feature 3" with `jj new -r 'description(substring:"Add feature 3")'`.

**Approach B â€” `jj diffedit -r <change_id>`:** Use `jj diffedit -r 'description(substring:"Add greeting")'` with a diff editor configured to apply the typo fix in place; descendants will be rebased automatically.

## Notes on jj behavior
- When you `jj edit` away from an empty working-copy commit that has no other descendants or bookmarks, jj may automatically abandon that empty commit. This is normal and expected â€” just make sure your final `@` ends up on or above the "Add feature 3" commit so all four files are visible in the working copy.
- The change id of the rewritten "Add greeting" commit stays the same (change ids are stable across rewrites); only the commit id changes.

## Constraints
- Project path: `/home/user/myrepo`
- The repository is colocated (`.git` + `.jj` both present); do not re-initialize it.
- Do NOT change any of the four commit descriptions.
- Do NOT remove or duplicate any of the four named commits (the chain must remain exactly: "Add greeting" â†’ "Add feature 1" â†’ "Add feature 2" â†’ "Add feature 3", all ancestors of `@`).
- Do NOT use git commands; use only `jj` commands to rewrite history.
- Do NOT use `--restore-descendants` â€” descendants must be rebased so that the working copy also sees the fixed file.
truth:
# Verification Plan

## Setup
1. `cd /home/user/myrepo`

## Verification Steps
1. Confirm the four commits still exist as a linear ancestor chain of `@` with descriptions "Add greeting", "Add feature 1", "Add feature 2", "Add feature 3" by running `jj log -r 'description(substring:"Add greeting")::@' --no-graph -T 'description.first_line() ++ "\n"'` and asserting that all four descriptions appear in the correct order.
2. Confirm the typo is fixed at the OLDEST commit's snapshot by running `jj file show -r 'description(substring:"Add greeting")' greeting.txt` and asserting that stdout equals exactly `Hello World\n`.
3. Confirm the working-copy tip sees the fix: `jj file show -r @ greeting.txt` outputs `Hello World\n` (no typo).
4. Confirm `feature1.txt`, `feature2.txt`, `feature3.txt`, and `greeting.txt` are all present at `@` by running `jj file list -r @` and checking that all four files appear.
5. Confirm no commit in the named chain still contains the literal string `Helo World` in `greeting.txt`.
6. Confirm exactly one commit exists for each named description.

## Specific Test Data
- Repository path: `/home/user/myrepo`
- Target file: `greeting.txt`
- Required final content of `greeting.txt`: `Hello World\n`
- Commit descriptions (oldest â†’ newest): `Add greeting`, `Add feature 1`, `Add feature 2`, `Add feature 3`

## Expected Results
- `jj file show -r 'description(substring:"Add greeting")' greeting.txt` returns `Hello World\n` with exit code 0.
- `jj file show -r @ greeting.txt` returns `Hello World\n` with exit code 0.
- The literal substring `Helo World` no longer appears in `greeting.txt` at any of the four named commits.
- `feature1.txt`, `feature2.txt`, `feature3.txt`, and `greeting.txt` are all present in the working-copy tip.
- The four named commits are linear ancestors of `@` in the documented order, with exactly one commit per description.
